title: Chapter 28: The Lawyer
content: The helicopter comes back every nineteen minutes. I timed it on the second pass, thinking I was being precise, being the kind of person who measures things. By the fourth pass I was counting because the sound was the only structure the morning had.
Bly Manor is under siege.

Not the quiet kind. Not the Wingrave kind, with locked doors and silenced women. This is the loud, bright, modern kind. Satellite trucks line the road from the gate to the village — I counted eleven from this window before they blurred into a single metal spine along the hedge. Journalists cluster at the iron gates like mourners who've arrived at the wrong funeral. Some have tents. One group has a barbecue. The helicopter belongs to Sky News, according to the logo on its belly, and it circles the manor the way a hand circles a throat.

I am watching from the upstairs landing window, the one that faces east, the one where the light comes in hard and yellow after nine. My reflection ghosts over the lawn, over the trucks, over the tiny figures with their cameras pointed at the house as if it might stand up and confess. I look pale. I look like someone who hasn't slept, which is accurate. I look like someone's daughter.
The laptop on the bed behind me has four thousand and seventeen unread emails.

I walked away from it twice. The first time to boil water for tea I didn't drink. The second time to stand in the shower until the water turned cold, thinking the noise would drown out the helicopter. It didn't. Nothing drowns out a helicopter. They're designed that way.

Four thousand and seventeen emails. I asked for a lawyer on a livestream watched by two million people, and four thousand and seventeen people answered. Some were cranks. Some were law students with more enthusiasm than qualifications. Some were journalists fishing for a source, their subject lines slick with false familiarity — "Thinking of you," "I can help," "URGENT and CONFIDENTIAL." Some were threats. Those I recognized by now. The internet has been threatening me for sixteen hours and has developed a consistent idiom.
But one email was eighteen lines long, written in plain English, and signed with a name I had to Google.

Sarah Chen, QC. Queen's Counsel. Ranked first in human rights law by Chambers and Partners for three consecutive years. Called to the Bar in 2003. Led the Hesketh inquiry. Led the Al-Rashid deportation appeals. The photographs online show a woman in her late forties with reading glasses pushed up into short dark hair and the kind of still face that could be reading a menu or passing a death sentence and you wouldn't know the difference until she spoke.

Her email said: "I saw the broadcast. I can represent you and the girl. Pro bono. I'll need to speak with you both before I can assess the case, but my preliminary view is that you have grounds. Don't speak to the press. Don't post anything else. Don't delete anything. Contact me on Signal at the number below."

I downloaded Signal. I sent the message. Twenty-six minutes later, I'm sitting cross-legged on the bed with the laptop open and the encrypted call connected, and the still face from the photographs is looking at me through a webcam with the expression of a surgeon examining an X-ray.

"Let me be clear about what this isn't," Sarah Chen says. Her voice is lower than I expected, clipped, each word placed with the deliberation of a brick. "This isn't a movement. This isn't a social media campaign with a hashtag and a goal. This is a legal proceeding. It will be slow. It will be boring. It will be decided on evidence and statute, not on how many people felt something while watching a video. Do you understand the difference?"
"Yes."
"Say it back to me. What is this?"
"It's a legal proceeding."
"And what is it not?"
"It's not a movement."
She nods once. A brick laid. "Good. Now. Walk me through what you have."

I tell her. All of it. The Game. The Manor. Hannah and Owen. Peter Quint and the recordings. Rebecca Jessel's face on the screen, her shadow on the water. I tell her about Flora, about Miles, about the children who watched and the children who filmed and the child who walked into a lake while a camera rolled. I tell her about the Wingrave Trust and the money and the silence it bought. I tell her about the man I came here to find, the one who built the machine that ate all these women, and I say "my father" and the word tastes like something I should have chewed longer before swallowing.

Sarah Chen listens. She doesn't take notes. Her eyes move while I talk, tracking something invisible — constructing, I realize, not a story, but an argument. When I finish, there is a silence that lasts exactly long enough to be comfortable and no longer.

"Your strengths," she says. "You have documentary evidence of criminal negligence resulting in death. You have a minor who is willing to testify. You have the recording itself, which has been viewed by approximately two million people, which makes suppression functionally impossible regardless of any legal outcome. Those are not insignificant."
I feel something loosen in my chest. A knot I've been holding since I hit stop on that livestream.

"Your weaknesses," she says, and the knot refastens. "You have a documented psychiatric history. You engaged the Bly Manor household under a false identity. You filmed minors without their consent or the consent of their guardian. You broadcast that footage to the internet. You — and this is not a legal term, Miss Bly, but I want you to hear it plainly — you are an unsympathetic defendant on paper. A court could be persuaded that you are a troubled young woman who exploited a tragedy for attention. The prosecution will try. They will find your hospital records. They will find your online history. They will find every unstable thing you have ever done and they will lay it out like a diagnosis, and a jury will look at it and think: well, perhaps."
I open my mouth. Close it.
"You have nothing to say to that?"
"I'm thinking."

"Good. Think faster in future. Opposing counsel won't wait." She removes her glasses, polishes them on something off-camera, replaces them. The gesture is oddly human. "The Jessel death is our strongest angle. Criminal negligence. A duty of care existed — she was in Wingrave employ, on Wingrave property, under Wingrave supervision. The recording demonstrates that supervision was not merely absent but actively perverse. Henry Wingrave is personally liable if we can show he knew, or should have known, about the conditions in this house. The footage does that."
"Henry knew," I say. "He has to have known."
"What he has to have done and what we can prove he did are different countries with a guarded border. We'll need testimony from inside the house. The children. The staff."
"Flora will testify."

Sarah Chen's expression doesn't change, but something behind her eyes performs a calculation I can't follow. "Your friend has made herself the most famous fourteen-year-old in Britain. That's not safe. It's not safe for the case — the court may find her compromised by media exposure, manipulated by adults, unreliable. And it's not safe for her. You understand? A child in the public eye is a child with a target on her back. Every editorial, every comment, every TikTok analysis of her demeanor is a weapon someone will use. She needs protection. Real protection. Not retweets."
"I know."

"Make sure you do. Because here is what will happen. Henry Wingrave will retain the finest solicitors in the country. They will file motions. They will request continuances. They will attempt to suppress evidence, question admissibility, challenge standing. They will try the case in the court of public opinion before it ever reaches a courtroom. And they will start today. Probably within hours."
She was right about the timing. She was wrong about the scale.

The garden stretches to the lake the way it always does. Green to blue, blue to grey, the sky and the water trading places at the horizon. The air smells of wet grass and something else — something metallic, maybe the helicopter's exhaust, maybe my imagination mapping the day's strangeness onto every sensation.
Flora is sitting at the edge of the lake.

She's wearing the grey cardigan she sleeps in and a pair of Owen's Wellingtons that come up past her calves. Her hair is uncombed. Her hands are in the mud. She is pressing her palms flat into the earth at the waterline, pushing down until the mud swallows her fingers, and then pulling them out, examining the brown stains, and pressing them in again. A ritual. A small one. The kind you perform when your body needs something to do that your mind cannot.
I sit beside her. The ground is cold. The cold seeps through my jeans and I let it.

"I keep thinking about her," Flora says. She's not looking at me. She's looking at the lake. The same lake. The exact coordinates on which Rebecca Jessel walked forward and kept walking until the water closed over her head like a book. "She stood right here. And we were inside watching it on screen. Like a movie. It wasn't a movie."
"No," I say. "It wasn't."
Flora pulls her hands from the mud. She wipes them on the cardigan, leaving brown streaks on grey wool. "Do you think she knew we were watching?"

The helicopter passes overhead. Its shadow sweeps across the lake, breaks the surface into fragments, moves on. The water settles back into stillness. I can smell the lake now — the green, closing smell of it, the algae and the silt and the cold. I can hear the reeds brushing together where the bank curves, a whisper like cloth on cloth. I can feel the damp working into my socks.
"I think she knew someone would find out eventually," I say.

Flora nods. Small. Nearly invisible. "That's the worst part. Not the dying. The knowing that someone would see it and still — " She stops. She doesn't finish. She doesn't need to. Still nothing happened. Still the cameras rolled. Still the Game played on. The watching was not the rescue. The watching was the mechanism.
A bird calls from the willow behind us. The lake gleams. The helicopter makes its circuit. The world continues to be a surface.

"She was a real person," Flora says. "Before. Do you know what I mean? She had a job she was good at. She had a flat in London. She had people who called her Becca. And now she's — " Flora's voice frays at the edge, a thread pulled from cloth. "Now she's content."
The word lands in my stomach like a stone dropped in water.

Content. That's what the comments call her. Content. That's what the TikTok compilations are — Jessel content, Bly Manor content, true crime content. A woman who walked into a lake and died has been transubstantiated by the internet into material. Into engagement. Into something to be consumed and scrolled past and consumed again. The machine that ate her in life is still eating her in death, and it has a wider mouth now.
"I'm sorry," I say.

"Don't be sorry. Be useful." Flora says it without heat. She says it the way you'd remind someone to take the bins out. Practical. The grief is there but it's been organized, filed, put to work. She's fourteen. She shouldn't know how to do that.
She stands. The Wellingtons make a sucking sound as she pulls them from the mud. "The lawyer," she says. "Is she good?"
"She's the best."

"Then let her be the best. I'm going to go wash my hands." She walks back toward the house, her footprints filling with dark water behind her, the Wellingtons too big, the cardigan stained, her small figure framed for a moment between the lake and the manor wall — framed, I think, the way Jessel was framed, the way we're all being framed, by cameras we can't see and audiences we'll never meet.
Then she passes through the garden gate and is gone.
I go back upstairs. The laptop is still open. Four thousand and twenty-three emails now. I don't open the new ones. I open the comments on yesterday's broadcast instead.
This is a mistake, but I make it.

The comments are a country. They have their own geography. In the highlands: support, outrage, calls for justice, threads of genuine horror from people who watched Jessel walk into that water and felt something real crack inside them. In the lowlands: doubt, accusation, the forensic deconstruction of my face, my voice, my motives, my psychiatric record, which someone has found and posted, because of course they have. In the swamps: conspiracy. I'm an actress. Jessel is alive. It's all a stunt. The children are CGI. The manor is a set. Nothing is real because nothing on the internet is real, and if nothing is real then no one is responsible and no one died and the water closed over nothing and nothing and nothing.

And in the middle of the country, the capital city: a single story, assembling itself in real time. The story of Maeve Bly. Who she is. What she means. The girl who exposed Bly Manor. The girl who ended the Game. The girl who fought back.
I didn't come here to fight back.
I came here to find my father.

And I found him. I found what he built. A house where women's pain was recorded and catalogued and stored like wine in a cellar, aged to perfection, consumed at leisure. Henry Wingrave didn't just hurt these women. He turned them into an archive. He made their suffering searchable. He gave it metadata. And he called it the Game, because calling it what it was would have required admitting that the man who built it was not a mastermind but a mouth.

My father built the machine. But I — I gave it its biggest audience. Two million people watched Jessel die because I showed them. Because I thought the truth would set us free. Because that's what the story says, isn't it? The truth will set you free. But the truth walked into a lake, and the audience watched, and the algorithm served, and the machine ate her one more time.

I close the laptop. My hands are shaking. I press them flat against the bed, the way Flora pressed hers into the mud, looking for something solid, something real, something that doesn't dissolve the moment you look at it.

Downstairs, I can hear the kitchen sounds — Owen, probably, making something, the clatter of a world that persists in being domestic. The helicopter goes by again. Nineteen minutes. I've been counting.
My phone buzzes. Signal notification. From Sarah Chen.
I open it.
The message is five lines long.
"Henry Wingrave has filed an emergency injunction to seize all Bly Manor footage. Hearing in 48 hours. If he wins, every recording becomes his property and will be destroyed."
I read it twice. Then a third time. The words don't change. The deadline doesn't negotiate.
Forty-eight hours.
The Game is over. But the house always wins.
